title: Quiz: Quantum Control Waveform Design
description: Test your understanding of pulse shaping techniques, DRAG pulses, spectral analysis, and waveform optimization for quantum gates

questions.question: What is the primary trade-off when using faster (shorter duration) quantum control pulses?

options.text: Faster pulses consume more electrical power
Faster pulses have broader bandwidth, increasing the risk of leakage to non-computational states
Faster pulses require more expensive DAC hardware
Faster pulses are harder to generate with FPGAs

questions.explanation: Faster pulses have broader frequency bandwidth (bandwidth ~ 1/duration). This broader bandwidth can couple to the |2> state (leakage) and to neighboring qubits (crosstalk). The fundamental trade-off is between speed and spectral purity.

questions.question: In DRAG (Derivative Removal by Adiabatic Gate) pulse shaping, what is added to the quadrature (Q) channel?

options.text: A copy of the Gaussian envelope shifted in time
The time derivative of the in-phase envelope, scaled by the anharmonicity

questions.explanation: DRAG adds the derivative of the in-phase envelope to the quadrature channel: Q(t) = -beta/alpha * dA/dt, where beta is the DRAG coefficient (~1) and alpha is the anharmonicity. This derivative term cancels transitions to the |2> state, suppressing leakage.

questions.question: For a Gaussian pulse with sigma = 4 ns, what is the approximate frequency bandwidth (FWHM)?
questions.explanation: The bandwidth of a Gaussian pulse is approximately FWHM = 0.44/sigma. For sigma = 4 ns: FWHM = 0.44/(4e-9) = 110 MHz. This is an important consideration when qubits are spaced close in frequency.

questions.question: Why is a 'net-zero' flux pulse used for two-qubit gates with tunable transmons?

options.text: To avoid long-term flux bias drift that would shift qubit frequencies

questions.explanation: Net-zero flux pulses (positive then negative lobe with zero integral) avoid accumulating DC flux bias over time. Accumulated flux bias would cause qubit frequency drift, requiring more frequent recalibration. The bipolar pulse provides the same gate interaction while returning to zero net flux.

questions.question: The DRAG coefficient beta should always be set to exactly 1.0 based on theoretical calculations.
questions.explanation: False. While theory predicts beta ~ 1, the optimal value depends on the specific qubit, pulse duration, and other system parameters. The DRAG coefficient must be calibrated experimentally, typically using techniques like AllXY sequences. Practical values often deviate from 1.0.

questions.question: In cross-resonance (CR) gates for fixed-frequency transmons, why is an echo sequence (CR - X_target - CR) commonly used?

options.text: To cancel unwanted single-qubit terms and ZZ interactions
To reduce the microwave power required
To simplify the pulse calibration process

questions.explanation: The echo sequence in CR gates cancels unwanted single-qubit Z rotations and static ZZ interactions that would otherwise accumulate. The X pulse on the target qubit inverts the sign of certain error terms, so the second CR pulse cancels them. This improves gate fidelity significantly.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid reasons to truncate a Gaussian pulse at 3-4 sigma from the center? (Select all that apply)

options.text: To limit pulse duration and reduce decoherence
To fit the pulse in available waveform memory
To reduce computational complexity of pulse generation

questions.explanation: Options A and B are valid reasons. Truncation limits duration (reducing decoherence) and memory requirements. Option C is incorrect because truncation actually causes spectral leakage (though 3-4 sigma keeps it very small). Option D is incorrect because Gaussian generation is computationally simple regardless of truncation.

questions.question: What is the minimum recommended gate time for a transmon with 250 MHz anharmonicity to avoid significant leakage?
questions.explanation: A rule of thumb is that gate time should be at least 4-5 times the inverse anharmonicity to avoid significant leakage. For 250 MHz anharmonicity: 1/(250 MHz) = 4 ns, so minimum gate time is 4-5 x 4 ns = 16-20 ns. This ensures the pulse bandwidth is narrow enough to avoid exciting the |2> state.
